I'd actually like to go. Can. Not unless I'm willing to change the whole relationship. Can't do it. Because in 12 months the whole relationship will have changed. So as much as I might like to go play golf with that guy once a week, not if he's in my coaching group. Can't do it. Now occasionally it's worth doing. But understand what you're doing and go ahead and change the relationship. Don't kid yourself, don't kid the other person, don't fool anybody. We're now friends. And now all the rules have changed. Real hard to impose rules on friends. Familiarity and friendship. Mark Victor Hansen hasn't given me any money in 25 years. Got a little bit of money from him 25 years ago. Hadn't given me any money in 25 years. Ain't gonna give me any money. He would benefit him enormously to give me some money. Ain't gonna give me any money. Too familiar. Friends. Not day to day go to the hamburger joint to get our friends, but friends. Phone calls just to talk. Send stuff to each other just to get an opinion. Too familiar. Know each other too well. Know every word in both directions. Not gonna give me any money. Damn sure ain't gonna give me the kind of money I get now. Impossible. It's exactly. Never give me any money. Too familiar. Benefit him enormously to give me money. Never gonna give me any money. Not the only two I can think of. But there they are. And so if you let this happen, it will preclude consulting and coaching relationships with someone. And you should not waste your time taking somebody who you have an enormous friendship relationship with or an enormous amount of familiarity with and putting them into a coaching or a consulting relationship because you ain't gonna be able to keep control of them. And if you got them in there, you can't let it change to this unless you are willing to consciously, deliberately make the change. But don't think you can walk the tightrope because you can. So some tips. Restrict and carefully control social interaction. Politely decline most social invitations. And if you do socialize, socialize with them as a group, not individually. So if you really like people in your coaching group, great. You all want to go bowling together on Wednesday night? Terrific. But go as a group. Don't go with one of them. Don't go with two of them. Avoid obligation at all costs. You want people obligated to you. You do not want to be obligated to them. Age old advice about money. Same advice applies about obligation in general. My challenge is to have everybody who pays me a lot of money still feel over and above the money they pay me that they owe me, they are obligated to me and that I can call upon them for all manner of favors and assistance and free work and support and they still don't feel like they've discharged the obligation. The second part of that trick is for them to feel I owe them nothing. Now it's easy to control my feelings by the way. It's very hard to obligate me emotionally. But I can't have them thinking it either. It cannot be in their head. This does not happen by accident. This happens by giving this thought. So for example, somebody said when we were talking about you can't, they can't pay enough, right? So let's say somebody has just paid $100,000 for a project. And I now deliver the work product of the project. I want to make sure that they know that I set aside something else I could have done, I would have made $200,000 from or I stayed up all night in order to get it done or here's what you paid for but here's the extra four things. I went above and beyond and then if the project works and the project makes the money, I want to remind them, hey, you know, and sometimes I'll do it jokingly, sometimes I'll do it in fun, sometimes not. But I want to be reminding them, you know, $2 million last year. Guess I should have charged you more.
